name: Embalse de Castadón – circular from Ourense por los Castros de San Tomé
name_original: Embalse de Castadón – circular desde Ourense por los Castros de San Tomé
region_intro: Jogging routes around the Mio are characterized by diverse natural landscapes, including the Huron National Forest and the Au Sable River Valley. The region offers varied terrain, from flat paths to sections with uneven ground, small hills, and sandy or rooted surfaces. This environment provides a range of running experiences within a natural setting. Best jogging routes around the Mio The most popular jogging route is Embalse de Castadón – circular from Ourense por los Castros de San Tomé, a 7.1 miles (11.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 23 minutes to complete. This moderate route features varied terrain suitable for a steady run. Another top favourite among local runners is Mirador do Coto da Pena – circular from Cortegada, a difficult 14.3 miles (23.0 km) path. This route offers a challenging experience with significant elevation gain. Local runners also love the Birth of the Miño River - circular via Peña de Lagoa and Cruz de Meira, a 5.6 miles (9.0 km) trail leading through natural landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 10 minutes. Jogging around the Mio is defined by river valleys, forested areas, and varied terrain including sandy and rooted sections. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy paths to more challenging routes with elevation changes. The routes in Mio are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 16 reviews. More than 2000 runners have used komoot to explore Mio's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in the Mio area?
A: The Mio area offers a wide variety of running routes, with over 100 options available. These include 17 easy trails, 76 moderate trails, and 23 difficult trails, catering to all fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on jogging trails around Mio?
A: Jogging routes around Mio feature diverse terrain. You'll find everything from flat paths to sections with uneven ground, small hills, and sandy or rooted surfaces. Some trails, like the Mio Trail, are known for their sandy, rolling to hilly terrain with whooped sections, while others offer more consistent surfaces.

Q: Are there any long-distance running trails in Mio?
A: Yes, the Mio area is home to the extensive &lt;strong&gt;Michigan Shore-to-Shore Riding & Hiking Trail&lt;/strong&gt;, which passes directly through Mio. This 500-mile interconnected trail system provides countless miles for long-distance jogging or shorter, scenic runs through varied landscapes.

Q: Can I find family-friendly jogging routes in Mio?
A: Absolutely. Trails like the &lt;strong&gt;Island Lake Nature Trail&lt;/strong&gt; are noted for being family-friendly, offering an accessible and enjoyable experience for all ages. Many of the easier routes within the Huron National Forest are also suitable for families.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Mio?
A: Yes, many trails in the Mio area are pet-friendly, including the &lt;strong&gt;Island Lake Nature Trail&lt;/strong&gt;. It's always a good idea to keep your dog on a leash and check specific trail regulations, especially within the Huron National Forest, to ensure a pleasant experience for everyone.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while jogging in Mio?
A: Mio is rich in natural beauty. You can jog through the expansive &lt;strong&gt;Huron National Forest&lt;/strong&gt;, experience the unique pine barren landscape of the &lt;strong&gt;Mio Prairie&lt;/strong&gt;, or follow trails along the picturesque &lt;strong&gt;Au Sable River&lt;/strong&gt;. The &lt;strong&gt;Wolf Creek Trail&lt;/strong&gt;, for instance, offers pleasant views along the river and includes an overlook.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Mio area?
A: Yes, many of the trails in Mio are designed as loops or can be combined to create circular routes. The &lt;strong&gt;Wolf Creek Trail&lt;/strong&gt; is a 4-mile loop that follows the Au Sable River, providing a great option for a circular run.

Q: What do other runners think about the trails in Mio?
A: The running routes in Mio are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 16 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse natural settings, the variety of terrain, and the immersive experience within the Huron National Forest and along the Au Sable River.

Q: Where can I find parking for jogging routes in Mio?
A: Many state forest campgrounds, such as &lt;strong&gt;Mio Pond State Forest Campground&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;Muskrat Lake State Forest Campground&lt;/strong&gt;, offer direct access to trails and provide convenient parking options. Additionally, trailheads for routes like the Michigan Shore-to-Shore Trail often have designated parking areas.

Q: Are there any challenging running routes for advanced runners in Mio?
A: Yes, for experienced runners seeking a challenge, Mio offers 23 difficult trails. Routes like the &lt;strong&gt;Mio Trail&lt;/strong&gt; feature sandy, rolling to hilly terrain with whooped sections, and a mix of black dirt, sand, rocks, and roots, providing a demanding workout. Other advanced options include trails like &lt;strong&gt;Loud Creek Lazy 8 North&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;Leaky Hills&lt;/strong&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for jogging in Mio?
A: Mio offers great jogging opportunities throughout the year. Spring and fall provide pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery with changing foliage. Summer is also popular, though some trails might be sandy. Winter jogging is possible, but runners should be prepared for snow and ice conditions.

Q: Can I combine my run with birdwatching in Mio?
A: Absolutely! The Mio area is known for its rich wildlife. The &lt;strong&gt;Kirtland's Warbler Habitat&lt;/strong&gt; is a notable natural feature where you might spot this rare bird while jogging, especially during nesting season. Keep an eye out for deer, eagles, and turkeys throughout the Huron National Forest.
tips: Near the archaeological site of San Tomé are the giant pots of the Loña River. These formations are created by the movement of rocks by the action of river currents.

Site of great chronological and cultural complexity, due to the different archaeological elements that allow confirming different types of habitat and settlement.
Everything indicates that the beginnings are located in the middle of the 1st century B.C.
The settlement was strategically located on the mountain, with a dominant position over the surroundings.
